author: @Buzzhumma
text: How about a hula hoop but only half the size with a magnetic ball inside . Have coils on the outside and hold the hoop in your hand and move the hoop with a rhythm that gets the magnetic ball circulating . Each coil could be connected in parellel to create a reasonable output current that could charge some caps for leds to light or even a phone if you had to . It would take a fair bit of winding . You could also have more than one magnetic ball with 100mm distance between them ! That would increase the output a lot

Where it really is no hassle to carry something little  larger such as a pedal  or hand generator, like a weekend camper van,  or just day trip too beach situation  then those slightly larger device  would be fine.  For even larger rigs... like week(s) long and or live in campers; . then maybe a  tower to put a bigger wind power device up high,  is maybe better to supplement the roof solar.   

side not- complaint Ive read about with the larger wind ones  used by more RV-ers  on a tower  they tilt up aside their camper when parked for a few days/weeks...... is the noise......  apparently it is very irritating to some..... be it user... or nearby camping neighbor

author: @experimentalprogramming1758
text:
I am familiar with the mechanism that is employed here. I found it out when i threw a *THIN LONG RECTANGULAR" wooden making post off the side of a mountain. And it whirled away with a signature buzzing sound, as if a bit of magic began to also propel it as it began whirling due to the fluid air moving over its "PERFECTLY SQUARED" surface as it was pulled towards the earth. And prolonging its falling time extensively. 

I emphasize these points because I believe the square shape is actually essential in this mechanism. I believe it has to do with the flow stopping effect of flat squared surfaces, so the device would be given the most torque when the "blade" is designed in such a shape. I was thinking you could perhaps make a strong blade in and extended "+" shape and you could even go further in this experiment and add more interlocked long thing squared rectangular blades till it is an extended "*" shape, and see which number of blades worked best or if just the regular the regular old long thin sharply squared, rectangular wooden(etc) fence post perhaps works best after all? Id actually love to see that. And you should try throwing those fence posts from a high place. Hold it in the middle of the post and throw it horizontally. And prepare to be mildly amazed. Especially if you do it from very high up. As it will travel far off course of the fall sometimes. Some have demonstrated that effect by dropping spinning balls from high places too. But with this it is amplified. Much more float effect. Enjoy. You and "Primitive Technology" (the original guy, not the copy cats) are my favorite channels on YouTube. Well done by far! 👏

I use to build an ancient growling paddle noisemaker when I was a kid, they were lots of fun and I built one back in 2009 and demonstrated it to a bunch of adults who had never seen one?!, They thought it was absolutely amazing, I had a great laugh then and that's why your generator made me giggle, you should've seen the expression on their faces when it started to make the bass buzzing tone noise! They were absolutely struck dumb🤣 so aside from the electrical coolness you brought back a great memory for myself👍
🐝💫

author: @MetalAsFork
text:
If you had a pole attached to the string, you could hold it straight up and get a ton of velocity with very little input. If it was a pole taller than you, you could just anchor the bottom to the ground and gently rock the pole with one hand as the whizzer zipped around you.

You could also prevent the device from crashing into the ground more easily, maybe with some kind of reel to control how much slack was out.

It's a funny invention, but not that absurd.

replies.author: @MetalAsFork
replies.text:
@christopherd.winnan8701 A human, standing there like these fellas were in an open space. That's the intent of the design, no? A human/wind-powered generator?

Or do you mean what's a better device at the end of the string? Probably the ball.

replies.author: @MetalAsFork
replies.text:
Hehe.

To automate it you just leave it on the post or tripod or whatever to capture the available wind. The question is how to best add some human input to get more/faster charging. Swinging your arm around is exertive, but just gently rocking a long staff back and forth is not too hard.

Imagine a 9' paracord attached to a 10' pole. Now tie Robert's spherefan to the end of that rope, and just toss it out away from you. As you just stand there and rock that pole a few inches in the right rhythm, that spherefan will be humming pretty good.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: The motor has resistance in the windings and backEMF when rotating, both of these effects mean it does not act as a direct short. Also 'speed controllers' can regulate the voltage/current from capacitor to motor.

replies.author: @templeofleila
replies.text:
I guess that's what I'm looking for. A speed controller for a capacitor. Thanks!
@bikerfirefarter7280

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
Cool.
This "moving the turbine" concept is one Ive been harping on for a while.

The best way for this I think would be putting it on a pole, with a swing arm (bent wire poked in some conduit would do) that way once it gets going you just have to wiggle the pole a bit to keep it going.
-you made something like before, out of a kids toy.

My real notion is to put a bunch of these (or other turbine) on a central hub, like the fairground swing ride, in concept. (Or spinning one of those square laundry hanging poles in the yard)

Well balanced, that sort of thing takes little effort to get and keep going by spinning the hub or pole.
It seems that for the same effort of a handcrank on One, you could move Several and let the wind crank them.  It Seems like you could get more output bang for your input buck. Could be wrong, but its on my mind.

author: @ThatTweedle
text:
it really is a great concept, but I feel like your spiral blade wouldn't work as well as propellers... but notice I said FEEL.... your demonstration in yesterday's video proves that it works just as well as a propeller blade, but I guess it being in such a ball-like package it FEELS counterintuitive that the blades would spin at all...
you could always make a similar sized propeller based setup like that guy did and then compare the two, but I think yous design would win overall.... it would be lighter, have less resistance, more efficient, and less cumbersome to spin around your head.....
...also you could use a long pole with the string tied to one end and just place the other end on the ground, then get the thing spinning and you'd only have to move the pole back and forth to keep it going.... I think THAT would be the BEST way to use this, and I don't think it would work so well with that guys propeller blade and fin setup.... 
...and you could run the wires along with the string, but make the wires a bit long than the string so all the tension is on the string... BUT you would have to come up with a way to keep the wires and string from getting twisted, sort of like an electrically conductive ball bearing for the wires and a regular one for the string.. but I would use a thin steel cable so it doesn't break... and you could  attach the inner piece of the bearing to the tip of the pole and weld the cable to the outer piece.... but with that amount of strength you don't need to worry about having the battery on the end like you did....  it would be so much less tiring than a hand crank ... Ohhh I would LOVE to see you make this LOL ...

author: @jima3252
text: Energy in a capacitor is voltage squared * Farad's / 2. So 2.3 squared * 100 / 2 = 264.5 joules. Not bad for a couple minutes work. But the voltage regulator lm2596 needs 3 volts minimum for input. You could put 2 or more caps in series though. 2 of them in series would then have a combined capacitance of 50 Farad's. But at 4.6 volts would have twice the energy at 529 joules.
